reasoning: The 10-year Treasury yield (DGS10) closed at 4.23% on August 1, 2025, plunging more than 15 basis points following a significantly weaker-than-expected July jobs report (73k jobs added vs. 100k expected). Because 4.23% represents a sharp, panic-driven drop, historical mean-reversion suggests a bounce in the short term (oversold conditions). The FRED linear trend model natively outputs a 54% probability of the yield being higher by August 10th. For the 1-week horizon (Aug 10), I assign a 56% probability that yields will tick up as the initial shock subsides. By the 1-month mark (Sept 2), right before the next jobs report and the September Fed meeting, I expect yields to remain somewhat normalized but still under downward pressure from impending Fed rate cuts (53%). Looking further out to November 2025 and January 2026, uncertainty increases immensely: on one hand, Trump administration tariffs are stoking long-term inflation expectations (steepening the yield curve); on the other hand, a Fed easing cycle is firmly in place, and consensus bank surveys (like Bankrate) forecast the 10-year dropping modestly to around 4.14% by the end of 2025. Per instructions, my uncertainty converges heavily toward 0.50 for these longer horizons (0.50 for Nov, 0.48 for Jan).
